title: History of Peru (Norte Chico · Coast · Highlands · Titicaca)

stack.label: Caral-Supe / Norte Chico (c. 3000–1800 BCE)
stack.note: Oldest urban center in the Americas; monumental pyramids at Caral

stack.label: Preceramic coastal settlements
stack.note: Chilca, Paloma fishing communities

stack.label: Kotosh (c. 3000–1500 BCE)
stack.note: Temple of the Crossed Hands; early ceremonial architecture

stack.label: Chiripa (c. 1500–100 BCE)
stack.note: Early Titicaca Basin settlement; precursor to Tiwanaku tradition

stack.label: Cupisnique (1500–500 BCE)
stack.note: North-coast Chavín-related tradition; Huaca de los Reyes

stack.label: Paracas (c. 800–100 BCE)
stack.note: Famous for elaborate textiles and cranial surgery

stack.label: Chavín (c. 900–200 BCE)
stack.note: Chavín de Huántar; pan-Andean religious influence; jaguar and serpent iconography

stack.label: Pucará (c. 500–200 BCE)
stack.note: Titicaca Basin polity; stone sculpture tradition

stack.label: Virú / Gallinazo (200 BCE – 300 CE)
stack.note: Moche Valley predecessors; fortified settlements

stack.label: Moche (c. 100–700 CE)
stack.note: Huaca del Sol y de la Luna; elaborate ceramics; Sipán royal tombs

stack.label: Nazca (c. 100–700 CE)
stack.note: Nazca Lines geoglyphs; polychrome pottery; Cahuachi ceremonial center

stack.label: Lima Culture (c. 200–700 CE)
stack.note: Maranga and Pachacámac complexes; central coast hegemony

stack.label: Recuay (200 BCE – 600 CE)
stack.note: Ancash highlands; stone sculpture; warrior cult

stack.label: Tiwanaku (c. 550–1000 CE)
stack.note: Lake Titicaca highlands; Gateway of the Sun; raised-field agriculture

stack.label: Moche decline / Lambayeque (Sicán) (c. 700–1375)
stack.note: Successor to Moche; Batán Grande gold workshops; Naylamp dynasty legend

stack.label: Wari influence on south coast
stack.note: Wari administrative centers at Pachacámac and Nazca region

stack.label: Wari Empire (c. 600–1000)
stack.note: First Andean empire; road system prefiguring Inca; capital at Ayacucho

stack.label: Tiwanaku (continued)
stack.note: Tiwanaku and Wari: two parallel Middle Horizon empires; collapsed c. 1000

stack.label: Chimú Kingdom (c. 900–1470)
stack.note: Chan Chan capital (largest adobe city); conquered Lambayeque c. 1375

stack.label: Chincha Kingdom (c. 1000–1476)
stack.note: Major maritime trading polity; long-distance exchange with Ecuador

stack.label: Chanka Confederation
stack.note: Andahuaylas-based; rival of Cusco; defeated by Pachacuti c. 1438

stack.label: Kingdom of Cusco (c. 1200–1438)
stack.note: Early Inca polity; Manco Cápac legendary founder; regional power before Pachacuti

stack.label: Colla / Lupaca Kingdoms
stack.note: Aymara kingdoms in Titicaca Basin; post-Tiwanaku successor states

stack.label: Inca Empire / Tawantinsuyu (1438–1533)
stack.note: Pachacuti → Túpac Inca → Huayna Cápac; 10 million km²; Quechua lingua franca; mit'a labor system

stack.label: Spanish Conquest (1532–1542)
stack.note: Pizarro captures Atahualpa at Cajamarca (1532); fall of Cusco (1533)

stack.label: Neo-Inca State of Vilcabamba (1537–1572)
stack.note: Manco Inca's resistance state in jungle; last ruler Túpac Amaru executed 1572

stack.label: Viceroyalty of Peru (1542–1824)
stack.note: Lima as capital of all Spanish South America until 18th-c. Bourbon reforms

stack.label: Túpac Amaru II Rebellion (1780–1783)
stack.note: Indigenous-mestizo uprising; largest colonial rebellion; 100,000 dead

stack.label: Bourbon Reforms
stack.note: Viceroyalty split: New Granada (1717), Río de la Plata (1776); Intendancy system

stack.label: Protectorate of Peru (1821–1822)
stack.note: San Martín declares independence 28 Jul 1821

stack.label: Republic of Peru (1824–)
stack.note: Battle of Ayacucho (1824) secures independence; caudillo era follows

stack.label: War of the Pacific (1879–1884)
stack.note: Peru and Bolivia vs Chile; Peru lost Tarapacá, Arica, Tacna

stack.label: Guano Era & Civilismo
stack.note: Guano exports fund modernization 1840s–1870s; Civilist Party era

stack.label: Modern Peru
stack.note: Leguía oncenio (1919–1930), Velasco military reform (1968–1975), Sendero Luminoso (1980–2000), democratic era

footnotes: Caral-Supe (c. 3000–1800 BCE) is the oldest known civilization in the Americas, contemporary with Egyptian and Sumerian civilizations
Chavín de Huántar (c. 900–200 BCE) served as a pan-Andean pilgrimage center; its iconography spread across Peru, defining the Early Horizon
The Moche (100–700 CE) produced some of the finest ceramics in pre-Columbian America; the Sipán royal tombs rival Tutankhamun in richness
Wari and Tiwanaku (c. 600–1000) were parallel Middle Horizon empires — Wari centered in Ayacucho, Tiwanaku at Lake Titicaca — whose collapse led to regional fragmentation
The Chimú Kingdom built Chan Chan, the largest adobe city in the world (20 km²), before being conquered by the Inca c. 1470
Tawantinsuyu ('four parts together') stretched from Colombia to Chile — the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, administered via quipu record-keeping and the Qhapaq Ñan road network
The Neo-Inca State at Vilcabamba (1537–1572) maintained Inca resistance for 36 years; Túpac Amaru I was the last Sapa Inca, executed in Cusco's plaza
Túpac Amaru II's rebellion (1780–83) was the largest anti-colonial uprising in the Americas, involving 100,000+ participants across Peru and Bolivia
The Viceroyalty of Peru was initially the sole Spanish viceroyalty in South America (1542), governing from Panama to Patagonia before Bourbon-era splits
The War of the Pacific (1879–1884) cost Peru its southern nitrate-rich territories; the national trauma shaped Peruvian politics for decades
